channel.title: World news | The Guardian
channel.description: Latest World news news, comment and analysis from the Guardian, the world's leading liberal voice
channel.copyright: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026
channel.dc:rights: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026

item.title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC
item.description:
UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease

A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential exposure to the Ebola virus.

The UK resident was medically evacuated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Nadeem Badshah

item.title: Healthy diet too expensive for one in three people globally, UN report finds
item.description:
Global hunger declined for the third year in a row in 2025 but experts call for action to bring down costs of fruit, vegetables and dairy as report shows 2.69bn people can’t afford to eat well

One in three people cannot afford to eat a healthy diet, a new report has found, prompting calls for urgent action on food affordability.

There are 2.69 billion people unable to afford a diverse diet that meets their energy and nutrient needs, and it will take “immense” amounts of work to achieve the global target of zero hunger by 2030, the annual report from five UN agencies warned.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kat Lay Global health correspondent

item.title: São Tomé and Príncipe heads to polls in tense presidential election
item.description:
Incumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independent

Voters in São Tomé and Príncipe go to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of Africa’s least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.

According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African state’s elections, approximately 15% of whom live in the diaspora.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Eromo Egbejule in Abidjan

item.title: ‘We are preserving a tradition’: how Ghana’s sensationalist film posters became collectible art
item.description:
Hand-painted works are often wildly unfaithful to the movies they portray – reinterpretations that sometimes resulted in threats, insults and even physical attacks from viewers who felt duped

Sitting on his porch in Teshie near Accra, Heavy J dipped a brush into red oil paint and dabbed it carefully on to his canvas – a flour sack – adding blood to a knife being wielded by a man. Higher on the canvas, he had started on an outline of a skull.

Heavy J was creating a poster, but not as you might have expected for a horror film. Instead, it was for the animated fairytale The Little Mermaid. The man with the knife was not a killer but the film’s kind-hearted prince, Eric. The skull was also unrelated to the story. “We add more to make people interested,” said Heavy J, whose real name is Jeaurs Affutu.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Carlos Mureithi in Greater Accra

item.title: Seven Americans quarantining at Kenya Ebola facility after US travel ban, says aid group
item.description:
Aid workers are first known people to quarantine at facility, which sparked huge opposition in Kenya

Seven American aid workers who had been in Congo to fight the Ebola outbreak are quarantining at a new isolation facility in ⁠Kenya after the US government introduced travel ⁠restrictions, the head of a US charity ​employing them told Reuters.

The aid workers are the first known people to quarantine at the facility, which has sparked huge opposition in Kenya and is at the heart of a legal case in which a court has ordered the work to be ⁠suspended. Construction continued, however, according to US officials and satellite imagery reviewed by Reuters.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Reuters

item.title: Canada cancels joint bridge-opening celebration with US amid trade tensions
item.description:
Trump’s 50% tariff threat means there will be no ribbon-cutting celebration of Gordie Howe International Bridge

A planned joint US-Canada ceremony on Friday celebrating the opening of the Gordie Howe International Bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor, Ontario, is no longer expected to take place after Donald Trump announced a 50% tariff on most Canadian goods.

In announcing the tariffs, the president accused Canada of unfairly discriminating against US automobiles, alcohol and dairy products, escalating tensions with one of the United States’ closest allies.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Olivia Bowden and agencies

item.title: Nicaragua’s congress announces ‘work plan’ to suspend elections
item.description:
US secretary of state says international community should ‘join forces’ against authoritarian Ortega regime

Nicaragua’s congress, controlled by the country’s long-serving president, Daniel Ortega, has announced a “work plan” to implement the authoritarian leader’s order that the country should no longer hold elections.

The UN and the US strongly condemned Sunday’s announcement by Ortega, who has been in power since 2007 and, after a controversial constitutional change last year, now governs alongside his wife, Rosario Murillo, as “co-president”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Tiago Rogero South America correspondent

item.title: Sperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discover
item.description:
Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close

Sperm whales produce a specific pattern of clicking noises when they hear the noise of boats, researchers have found. The pattern is so distinct that the scientists who identified it said they can now use it to predict when ships are nearby.

Researchers from Project Ceti (the Cetacean Translation Initiative), a non-profit research organisation working to decode sperm whale communication, spent four years tuning in on the calls of 15 sperm whales off the coast of Dominica.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Emma Bryce

item.title: Trump announces new tariffs on generic drugs to take effect in 2028 – as it happened
item.description:
This live blog is now closed.

  • Sign up for US Breaking News email

US gas prices have continued to rise since the conflict with Iran resumed this month. According to the American Automobile Association (AAA), a gallon of fuel is now just over $4 – almost a dollar more than a year ago.

It comes as Brent Crude, the international oil benchmark, is now up by almost 2% to $90.92 a barrel.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Robert Mackey, Shrai Popat, Maya Yang and Tom Ambrose

item.title: Trump defends 50% tariffs on Canada, saying ‘they need us to survive’
item.description:
Mark Carney says Canada is ‘united’ in face of threats as leaders agree to talk before tariffs take effect next month

  • Sign up for the Breaking News US newsletter email

Donald Trump has defended his new 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, claiming the country cannot survive without the US.

The White House announced the sweeping round of import duties – due to come into effect next month – on Monday, igniting fresh tensions between Washington and Ottawa.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Shrai Popat in Washington

item.title: British woman jailed in Hong Kong for blackmail after accusing banker of rape
item.description:
Isabel Rose, 26, sentenced to six years for blackmail and perverting course of public justice over her allegations against British man

A British woman who reported being raped to police in Hong Kong has been sentenced to six years in prison for blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

Isabel Rose, 26, from south-east London, travelled to Hong Kong in early 2024 to visit a British banker she had met while travelling in south-east Asia.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Danny Vincent

item.title: Australia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenal
item.description:
Foreign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Penny Wong will warn China that Australia and other neighbouring nations will not be bullied by its “provocative” actions, after its involvement in another “destabilising and dangerous” incident this week.

The foreign affairs minister – who will deliver the address on Thursday in the Philippines, where the 59th Asean foreign minister’s meeting is being held – will also caution world leaders against placing a toll on the strait of Hormuz after both Iran and the US threatened to do amid the protracted conflict.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sarah Basford Canales and Luca Ittimani

item.title: Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail over ant dessert
item.description:
Prosecutors seek a one-year sentence and heavy fine after chef garnished dish with insects not approved for consumption

Prosecutors in South Korea have asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant to one year in prison for serving a dessert topped with ants, an insect not approved for consumption under local food safety law.

The request, made on Monday, also sought a 20m won (£10,108) fine for the company that runs the restaurant, Yonhap news agency reported.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea

item.dc:creator: Raphael Rashid

item.title: Japan records its first ‘cruelly hot day’ as cities swelter in 40-degree heat
item.description:
In April Japan coined new term kokushobi for days above 40C as country advises workers to wear shorts to the office to cope with stifling heat

Japan logged its first official kokushobi or “cruelly hot day” on Tuesday since the new term for when temperatures top 40 degrees Celsius was introduced in April. The Japan Meteorological Agency coined the new designation to warn the public of the heat dangers of days that were once freak weather events but have now become more common.

The central Japan cities of Gujo and Tajimi in Gifu prefecture, along with Toyota in Aichi prefecture, all hit 40C. Gujo’s 40C and Toyota’s 40.1C were all-time highs recorded at their respective weather stations.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Gavin Blair in Tokyo

item.title: Australia spent more than $9m for each asylum seeker held on Nauru last year – so why can’t they afford to buy food?
item.description:
Many of the more than 100 people awaiting processing on the tiny Pacific island are skipping meals to make ends meet, a new survey has found

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Most refugees and asylum seekers held in Nauru’s detention centres say they cannot afford to buy enough food or access clean drinking water, despite the Australian government spending the equivalent of more than $9m per person last financial year.

The group of more than 100 people have been sent indefinitely to the tiny Pacific island while they wait for their asylum claims to be processed. Many of the cohort say they are skipping meals to make ends meet, according to a new survey by the Asylum Seeker Resource Centre (ASRC) advocacy group.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sarah Basford Canales

item.title: Australia news live: Brisbane fog causes flight delays; Labor faces seven major challenges, Chalmers says
item.description:
Treasurer says ‘parties of the sensible centre and the centre-left parties’ face ‘serious economic challenges’. Follow live

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Self-reported domestic violence incidents declining in NSW but deaths and hospitalisations fail to fall

New data shows self-reported domestic violence incidents in New South Wales have declined, but the number of women and children murdered or hospitalised in domestic and family violence incidents over the past eight years has not significantly budged.

The decline in self-reported violence is significant because it reflects underlying levels of violence in the community, not just what is reported to police. It suggests that, despite rising reports of domestic violence to police, domestic violence in the community is not increasing and may be trending down.

There’s a strong perception that violence against women is rising, which is understandable given increases in police-recorded incidents, heightened media attention and greater public awareness.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Lauren Plews/Sanctuary Cove Concierge & Transfers
Photograph: Lauren Plews/Sanctuary Cove Concierge & Transfers
Photograph: Lauren Plews/Sanctuary Cove Concierge & Transfers

item.dc:creator: Caitlin Cassidy

item.title: AusAlert test will blast emergency warning siren over most phones in Australia on Monday. Here’s what to know
item.description:
National emergency test alert will be sent to all mobile devices on 27 July, with domestic violence advocates advising how to turn off safe phones

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Virtually every phone in the country will blare an emergency siren at 2pm AEST on Monday 27 July as part of the first Australia-wide test of a new national warning system called AusAlert.

Most phones, and some smartwatches and tablets, will receive a pop-up notification and an alert sound that cannot be silenced – even if your device is on “do not disturb” or silent mode.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Josh Butler

item.title: Why are OpenAI and Anthropic cheering on regulation in Australia? The answer has global reach
item.description:
The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Top US AI developers Anthropic and OpenAI cheered when Australia announced it would set new AI rules.

Big tech celebrating limits on their Silicon Valley VC-funded free-for-all might seem counterintuitive but there’s a much broader play than just what happens in one relatively small market.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Luca Ittimani

item.title: How will Australia’s emergency services cope if satellites go down? With a sextant and a street directory
item.description:
Military and SES personnel being taught analog communication and navigation techniques as experts warn solar flare could knock out ‘critical space infrastructure’

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Australia’s emergency services and military personnel are being trained to read paper maps, navigate without GPS support and sail by the stars in case something knocks out the nation’s space capabilities.

A panel at the 19th Australian Space Forum in Adelaide on Wednesday was asked to consider what would happen “if we suddenly lost access to critical space infrastructure – no satellites, no GPS, no remote sensing, no secure communications?”

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Tory Shepherd

item.title: Great Barrier Reef microbiome mapped as researchers discover 500 bacterial species previously unknown to science
item.description:
Research identifies 808,585 viral genomes belonging to what scientists estimate to be 362,802 different viruses

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

More than a decade after the human microbiome was comprehensively mapped, scientists have now identified the vast communities of microbes that make up the Great Barrier Reef.

Analysing DNA from seawater samples of 48 reefs, researchers have mapped the microbiome of the Great Barrier Reef, documenting more than 360,000 distinct viruses as well as more than 500 new bacterial species not previously known to science.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Donna Lu

item.title: Zelenskyy under growing pressure to reinstate sacked defence minister
item.description:
Campaigners in Ukraine pledge to continue nationwide protests until Mykhailo Fedorov gets his job back

Volodymyr Zelenskyy was under growing pressure to reinstate his sacked defence minister on Wednesday, after campaigners said they would continue indefinite nationwide protests until Mykhailo Fedorov got his job back.

On Tuesday, Zelenskyy bowed to public demands by firing Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi. He appointed Mykhailo Drapatyi as the new head of the country’s armed forces, as part of a radical reshuffle of the top military command.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Luke Harding in Dnipro

item.title: New Ukraine army chief vows to step up counteroffensive as EU bids to finalise fresh Russia sanctions – as it happened
item.description:
Brussels talks between senior diplomats now underway in attempt to sign off 21st package of sanctions, after previous failed attempts

The Venice Biennale said it would challenge a European Union decision to withdraw a €2 ⁠million grant in response to the return of Russia to its 2026 edition, despite continuing aggression on Ukraine, Reuters reported.

The Italian government sharply criticised the move, while the European Commission warned it ​would suspend or terminate funding if the Russian ‌pavilion returned.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jakub Krupa

item.title: France records more than 5,700 excess deaths during June heatwave
item.description:
Figures come as large parts of southern Europe endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperatures

France recorded more than 5,700 excess deaths during its June heatwave, authorities have said, as swathes of southern Europe continue to endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperatures.

The French national health agency said on Wednesday that it had recorded 5,764 excess deaths ⁠between ​17 June and 2 July, an excess mortality rate of 36%.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jon Henley, Europe correpondent

item.title: ​Andy Burnham’s ​domestic ​revolution ​could ​unobtrusively pull Britain ​closer to Europe
item.description:
The new PM talks less about the continent than his predecessor, but his agenda is more closely aligned with the EU’s

• Don’t get This Is Europe delivered to your inbox? Sign up here

Europe’s leaders queued up this week to welcome Andy Burnham as Britain’s seventh prime minister since the Brexit vote – all expressing the hope that his tenure would continue the thaw in EU-UK relations initiated by his predecessor, Keir Starmer.

Ursula von der Leyen, the European Commission chief, said she was looking forward to strengthening cross-Channel ties, adding that a “very good” first discussion had confirmed a common commitment to “bringing the EU and UK closer and closer”. France’s Emmanuel Macron said he hoped for a deeper relationship that gave “new momentum to the partnership” between the EU and the UK. Germany’s Friedrich Merz said he, too, was looking forward to forging an even closer bond.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jon Henley

item.title: Food price fears after Europe’s heatwave destroys estimated €2bn of crops
item.description:
Record June temperatures expected to cut grain harvest by 9m tonnes to its lowest level since 2018

The heatwave that swept across Europe in June is estimated to have cost the continent’s grain farmers €2bn (£1.7bn) in lost revenue and destroyed 9m tonnes of crops, sparking concern about the impact on the price of food.

Coceral, the European cereals and oilseeds trade association, made the estimate for the harvest of crops such as wheat, barley, maize and oats following the heatwave.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Joanna Partridge

item.title: Yemen’s Houthis claim attacks on Red Sea tankers as US launches 12th night of strikes on Iran – Middle East crisis live
item.description:
US military says it is degrading Tehran’s ability to threaten ships in strait of Hormuz; Houthis say tankers violated their blockade; US signs nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

More on Yemen’s Houthis claiming strikes on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea:

The master of one tanker reported being struck by an unknown projectile about 70 nautical miles south-west of Al Shuqaiq, causing a fire that the crew was fighting, the British naval security monitor UKMTO said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Adam Fulton

item.title: Iran warns of ‘eye for an eye’ response if US follows through on Trump’s threats to destroy infrastructure – as it happened
item.description:
This live blog is now closed.

The Jordanian army said it intercepted four Iranian missiles on Wednesday with two additional missile falling in an open area, AFP reports.

The two missiles that the army did not intercept fell in “two remote, uninhabited areas, without causing any human casualties or material damage” the army said in a statement.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Lucy Campbell (now); Hayden Vernon, Tom Ambrose, Vivian Ho and Mark Saunokonoko (earlier)

item.title: Mamdani says he can’t arrest Netanyahu as mayor and urges US to enforce ICC warrant
item.description:
Mamdani admits he lacks power to arrest Israeli prime minister after saying he’s ‘not welcome’ in New York City

Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, doubled down on his support for the international criminal court (ICC) on Wednesday and called on federal authorities to execute the court’s warrant for Benjamin Netanyahu’s arrest should he visit the city in the fall.

“I call on the federal government to join the international criminal court and to execute both this warrant as well as any warrant the international criminal court holds,” Mamdani told reporters at a press conference on Wednesday.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Alice Speri

item.title: Mamdani urges US government to arrest Netanyahu if Israeli PM visits US
item.description:
New York mayor admits he does not have authority to arrest Netanyahu, adding Israeli PM is ‘architect of a horrific genocide’

Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does not have the authority to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year.

“Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” Mamdani said in a video posted to social media.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Robert Mackey

item.title: Wednesday briefing: How Trump’s Iran gamble has produced a crisis reshaping the global order
item.description:
In today’s newsletter: ​A White House operating without expertise, strategy or restraint ​is stuck in a conflict with no easy way out, which is sending economic shockwaves far beyond its own borders

Good morning. Within two hours of entering No 10 on Monday afternoon, Andy Burnham had his first call with Donald Trump. After chit-chatting about the football (both agreed England had an “excellent tournament”) and Manchester (our new prime minister invited the president to visit the city Trump once described as a “town”), they got on to far trickier territory: the strait of Hormuz.

The US has attacked Iran for the eleventh consecutive night, hitting aircraft hangars and drone storage sites across the country. It came after Iran launched retaliatory strikes on US air bases and neighbouring Gulf states, which have accused it of deliberately targeting desalination plants that are essential to life in the region. Each side now claims it is preventing the other from gaining control over the strait of Hormuz, through which 20% of the world’s oil was shipped before the war began. Vessels carrying crucial shipments of oil, gas and fertiliser remain at a standstill.

UK politics | Andy Burnham is under mounting pressure to explain how he will pay for his policy pledges as he implored his new cabinet to examine “all possible ways” to tackle the cost of living crisis.

UK news | A man has appeared in court charged with the murder of the former MP and Reform UK spokesperson Ann Widdecombe. Joshua Kerry, 28, is accused of killing the former Conservative minister at her home in Haytor, Devon, by hitting her on the head with a hammer 21 times.

Middle East | The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, told the Senate the US-Israel war on Iran had cost $37.5bn so far, as he faced lawmakers furious over the conflict’s soaring price tag and the deaths last week of three American service members.

Climate crisis | About 23 million people will be banned from using hosepipes by this weekend, as England and Wales moves closer to drought status.

Ukraine | Volodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hettie O'Brien

item.title: Opposition parties shut down Indian parliament in solidarity with ‘cockroach’ protests
item.description:
Proceedings suspended as politicians call for Modi government to address police violence and education scandals

India’s opposition parties have shut down parliament in a protest calling for the resignation of the education minister, in solidarity with the new youth-led “cockroach” movement.

Opposition politicians, led by the Congress party leader Rahul Gandhi, gathered outside the parliament building in Delhi on Wednesday, many dressed in black and waving placards calling on Dharmendra Pradhan to quit.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: India’s Cockroach movement protesters show no sign of backing down
item.description:
Thousands of people again on the streets of Delhi calling for education reforms despite police violence on Monday

Taru had come to the protest prepared this time. In one hand was an anti-government poster and in the other was a bicycle helmet. “We need to have protection in case Delhi police beat us up again,” said the 25-year-old, who stood among her friends. “We are not scared of them and we won’t run away, no matter what they do. We’ll stay here till they listen.”

On Tuesday, central Delhi still thronged with thousands of protesters, as an unprecedented expression of dissent against the powerful ruling Narendra Modi government showed no sign of abating.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: Rescuers in Afghanistan search for missing and dead after flash floods destroy homes
item.description:
National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in eastern province of Nuristan

Rescue crews in Afghanistan are searching for survivors and bodies among the rubble of houses destroyed by devastating flash floods that authorities said had killed nearly two dozen people and left more than 100 people missing.

The Afghanistan national disaster management authority said on Tuesday that 23 people had died, 80 had been injured and more than 100 were missing after floods struck the eastern province of Nuristan on Monday, causing an undetermined number of buildings to collapse. The provincial capital city of Parun was among the areas affected, authorities said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Associated Press in Kabul

item.title: Tens of thousands of Cockroach movement protesters defy police with Delhi march
item.description:
Youth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in years

Tens of thousands of protesters have defied police orders and descended on Delhi to demand the resignation of India’s education minister in one of the largest anti-government demonstrations in years.

A huge crowd, largely made up of students and gen Z-ers, filled the streets in the heart of India’s capital from early Monday morning shouting anti-government slogans. Hundreds had already camped out overnight.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: ‘When I get home, my whole body aches’: extreme heat saps health and wages of India’s poorest workers
item.description:
Exclusive: Outdoor workers losing up to 9% of income as weather becomes ‘poverty multiplier’, research says

Extreme heat is pushing some of India’s poorest workers further into poverty, with income losses of up to 9% for outdoor workers, new research has found.

Analysis done by the International Institute for Environment and Development (IIED), and shared exclusively with the Guardian, found that extreme heat is acting as a “poverty multiplier” for hundreds of millions of workers in India’s informal sector.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Carmen Aguilar García and Antonio Voce in London; Hannah Ellis-Petersen and Aakash Hassan in Delhi

item.title: One UK mosque attacked ‘every five days’, says British Muslim Trust
item.description:
More than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalism

One mosque is attacked in Britain every five days, according to new data released by the government’s partner for monitoring anti-Muslim hatred.

More than 70 attacks on mosques in the UK have been recorded over the past year, which include acts of intimidation, violence, property damage, harassment, anti-Muslim hate speech, online abuse and vandalism.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Aamna Mohdin and Jayni Makwana

item.title: Scottish Labour’s rivals decry ‘chancer’ Anas Sarwar for resigning to join Andy Burnham’s government
item.description:
New prime minister has made Sarwar – the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer’s resignation – a peer

Scottish Labour’s rivals in Holyrood have slammed Anas Sarwar’s shock decision to resign as leader to sit in Andy Burnham’s new government, calling him a “chancer” and accusing him of lying to voters.

The prime minister has made Sarwar – who was the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer to resign – a peer and given him a ministerial post at the Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade, No 10 announced on Wednesday.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Robyn Vinter

item.title: Paramount’s £80bn takeover of Warner Brothers a step closer after EU approval
item.description:
European Commission says Paramount Skydance’s decision to end Universal venture addresses merger concerns

Paramount Skydance’s £80bn takeover of Warner Brothers took a step closer to being realised after it won conditional approval from the European Union.

The European Commission said Paramount’s offer to ⁠end a film ⁠distribution ​joint venture with Universal Pictures addressed its concerns over the potential merger. As part of its commitments in the EU, Paramount has 13 months from the date of closing the deal to pull out from the venture.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hayden Vernon

item.title: Burnham stirs up political row with review of release plans for violent criminals
item.description:
PM says he wants to ensure ‘everything has been looked at’ amid outrage at policy from family of PC Andrew Harper

Andy Burnham is immersed in a deepening political row after ordering a review of plans for the early release of thousands of killers, rapists and sex offenders from prison in September.

After the family of PC Andrew Harper expressed outrage that his killers may be eligible for release, the prime minister responded by pledging to make sure that “everything has been looked at again” before a fresh tranche of about 6,000 offenders were freed on tags.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Rajeev Syal and Alexandra Topping

item.title: Woman, 70, charged with murder of British expat Karen Carter in France
item.description:
Public prosecutor for the region said suspect had been initially arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last year

A 70-year-old woman has been charged with the murder of Karen Carter, the British expat found dead in south-western France last year.

The woman was charged on 2 July with Carter’s murder in Trémolat, in the Dordogne, and is “under judicial supervision”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jamie Grierson and Kim Willsher

item.title: House Democrat calls for congressional hearings on Trump administration’s nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia – US politics live
item.description:
Brad Sherman, US representative from California, said Congress should approve agreement only if ‘gold-standard safeguards’ are included

  • US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

  • Sign up for the Breaking News US email

A reminder that my colleagues are covering the latest out of the Middle East at our dedicated live blog.

Donald Trump took to Truth Social a short while and noted that the US will destroy one bridge or power plant, including those near Tehran, “any time” that Iran “shoots a ship” in the strait of Hormuz.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Robert Mackey (now); Shrai Popat, Lucy Campbell and Tom Ambrose (earlier)

item.title: Possible bloodstains were detected at D4vd’s home, law enforcement testify
item.description:
Gory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas Hernandez

At singer D4vd’s former Hollywood Hills home, law enforcement detected possible bloodstains – invisible to the naked eye – on items including a green tarp, black rubber mats and a crumpled inflatable pool that had the odor of fresh laundry.

The gory details came to light on Wednesday, the second day of a hearing to determine whether there was probable cause for the musician, whose full name is David Anthony Burke, to stand trial for the murder and sexual abuse of Celeste Rivas Hernandez.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Uwa Ede-Osifo in Los Angeles

item.title: Trump scoffs at ‘affordability’ as he touts economic policies at Georgia rally
item.description:
President visits swing state to promote ‘Trump accounts’ and says high prices lingering from Joe Biden’s presidency

Donald Trump scoffed at the word “affordability” as a political message, describing it at a campaign rally on Wednesday as a term concocted by Democratic political consultants to criticize his presidency. Yet even as the president dismissed the notion of affordability, he devoted much of his speech at Wheeler high school to touting the bread-and-butter benefits of his economic policies.

“So I just want you to remember that if you’re paying a little bit more – a lot of it’s come down now, we got it coming down very rapidly actually,” Trump said in Marietta, an Atlanta suburb, casting higher prices today as a lingering effect of the Biden administration.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: George Chidi in Marietta, Georgia

item.title: Tesla’s profits slide despite growing revenue as it pivots to robotics and AI
item.description:
Shares in Elon Musk company fall more 3% in after-hours trading, as earnings per share miss Wall Street expectations

Tesla reported its second-quarter earnings on Wednesday, disclosing far lower profits than expected. The company’s already beleaguered stock, which had fallen about 14% this year to date, dipped further following the earnings report.

Elon Musk’s automaker, once the pinnacle of his tech empire, has taken a back seat to SpaceX. Musk’s rocket and AI company held the largest stock market debut in history last month, turning the richest man on Earth into the world’s first trillionaire, though his net worth has since fallen from its peak.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Nick Robins-Early

item.title: House Republicans to write $95bn bill to fund Iran war and aid farmers
item.description:
GOP-controlled chamber paves way for reconciliation bill, while making long-shot attempt at new voting restrictions

House Republicans on Wednesday agreed to begin the process of writing a $95bn bill to fund the war with Iran as well as provide new aid to farmers, while making a long-shot attempt to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide.

In a virtually party-line 216-214, the Republican-controlled chamber passed a budget resolution that paves the way for the creation of a reconciliation bill, which can advance through the Senate under special rules that circumvent the Democratic filibuster and requires only a simple majority to pass. Two Republicans – Warren Davidson of Ohio and Thomas Massie of Kentucky, as well as Kevin Kiley, an independent of California who aligns with the GOP – joined all Democrats in opposing the measure.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Chris Stein
